question: Explain the NgRx flow: store, action, reducer, selector, effect.
answer:
A Redux pattern with one-way data flow:

  • Store — single immutable source of truth.
  • Action — a plain event describing what happened.
  • Reducer — pure function (state, action) → new state, no side effects.
  • Selector — memoised query to read a slice of state.
  • Effect — handles side effects (API calls); listens for an action, does async work, dispatches success/failure.

Component dispatches → effect calls API → dispatches success → reducer updates store → selector pushes the new value to the component.
tip: Draw the loop on the whiteboard — dispatch → effect → reducer → selector.
deep:
The loop, in order

Draw this — interviewers ask for the whiteboard version:

Component --dispatch(action)--> Store
                                  |
                        +---------+---------+
                        |                   |
                    Reducer             Effect
                (pure, sync)        (side effects, async)
                        |                   |
                   new state          dispatch(successAction)
                        |                   |
                    Selector <--------------+
                        |
                   Component (async pipe)

Key subtlety: an action goes to the reducer and the effects — simultaneously, not one then the other. Reducers run first (synchronously), then effects react. Both see every action.

The five pieces and their one job

PieceJobRule
Storesingle source of truthone immutable state tree
Actiondescribes what happenedan event, never a command
Reducer(state, action) => newStatepure, sync, no I/O
Selectorquery/derive statepure, memoised
Effecteverything impureaction in, action out

Why the indirection exists

The point is one-way data flow. State can only change by dispatching an action, so every change is described by a serialisable object. That single constraint buys you:

  • Traceability — DevTools shows an ordered log of everything that happened.
  • Time-travel — replay actions from initial state to reproduce any bug.
  • Testability — reducers are pure functions; no mocks needed.

The price is boilerplate. That trade-off is the answer to "why NgRx?" — say both halves.

Actions are events, not commands

// ✘ command — a caller telling the store what to do
addUserToCart({ user })

// ✔ event — a fact about what happened
'[Product Page] Add To Cart Clicked'

Model actions as unique events from a source, not as setters. One event can drive several reducers and effects; a command implies exactly one handler and quietly recreates tight coupling.

Where beginners get it wrong

  • Calling the API in the reducer — reducers are pure and sync; that's what effects are for.
  • Reusing one action across many places to "save code" — you lose traceability, which was the entire point.
  • Putting derived data in the store — derive it in a selector instead, or it goes stale.

How to say it in an interview

“A component dispatches an action; the reducer folds it into new state purely and synchronously, and effects react to the same action for anything async like HTTP, dispatching a success or failure action that flows back through a reducer. Components read via memoised selectors. The whole point is that state only changes through a serialisable action, which is what gives you the DevTools log and time-travel debugging — and the cost is the boilerplate, which is why I wouldn't use it on a small app.”

question: When would you NOT use NgRx?
answer: NgRx adds boilerplate. Skip it for small apps or local state — a simple service with a BehaviorSubject (or Signals) is enough. Reach for NgRx when state is large, shared across many features, and needs traceability / undo / debugging.
tip: The senior answer is 'right tool for the size of the app', not 'always use NgRx'.
deep:
The honest answer: most of the time

This question is a judgement test. Candidates who answer "always use NgRx, it's best practice" fail it. NgRx is a specialised tool with a real cost, and knowing when to decline it is the seniority signal.

Don't use NgRx when…

  • The state is local. Dialog open, selected tab, form value — that's component state. Putting it in a global store is a global variable with ceremony.
  • You're just caching HTTP. If the "state" is really server data you fetch and display, a service with shareReplay(1) — or TanStack Query — does it in 5 lines. NgRx is not a caching library.
  • It's a small/medium app or a small team. The conventions pay off across many features and many developers. Below that, they're just typing.
  • Only one feature uses it. If nothing else reads it, it isn't global state.

Do use NgRx when…

  • State is shared across many features and updated from several places.
  • You need traceability — an audit log of what changed and why (DevTools, time-travel).
  • Complex async coordination — racing, cancelling, retrying, sequencing across features.
  • A large team that benefits from one enforced way of doing things.
  • Undo/redo, or optimistic updates with rollback.

The ladder — the answer that lands

NeedReach for
Local UI statesignal() / a component field
Shared across a few componentsservice + signal / BehaviorSubject
Server cacheshareReplay(1) in a service
Feature-scoped complex stateNgRx SignalStore / ComponentStore
Large shared app state + traceabilityfull NgRx Store

Naming SignalStore matters in 2026 — it's the modern middle ground: most of NgRx's structure with a fraction of the boilerplate, and no actions/reducers unless you want them. A candidate who only knows classic Store sounds a few years out of date.

The cost, concretely

One API call in classic NgRx = 3 actions + a reducer case + an effect + a selector, across 4 files. That's the tax. It buys structure and traceability; it doesn't buy correctness or speed.

How to say it in an interview

“I'd skip it for local UI state or when I'm really just caching server data — a service with shareReplay handles that. NgRx earns its boilerplate when state is genuinely shared across features, updated from multiple places, and I need the traceability and time-travel to debug it, or with a big team that benefits from one enforced pattern. In between, I'd reach for SignalStore now — most of the structure, far less ceremony.”

question: Why must reducers be pure and immutable?
answer: Purity (same input → same output, no side effects) makes state predictable, testable, and time-travel debuggable. Immutability (return a new object, never mutate) lets the store and OnPush components detect changes by reference, keeping change detection fast.
tip: Use the spread operator or an entity adapter — never push/splice existing state.
code: on(loadSuccess, (state, { items }) => ({ ...state, items, loading: false }))
deep:
Pure = same input, same output, no side effects

// ✘ NOT pure — I/O, and a different result each call
on(load, (state) => {
  this.http.get('/users').subscribe();   // side effect!
  return { ...state, at: Date.now() };   // non-deterministic!
});

// ✔ pure — a value in, a value out
on(loadSuccess, (state, { users }) => ({ ...state, users, loading: false }));

Banned in a reducer: HTTP, routing, localStorage, Date.now(), Math.random(), console.log. All of it belongs in an effect.

Why purity buys you time-travel

DevTools replays state₀ + [action₁, action₂, …] to reconstruct any point in time. That only works if replaying an action always produces the identical state. One Date.now() and the replay diverges from what actually happened — the feature silently breaks.

Why immutability is about change detection

// ✘ mutation — the reference is UNCHANGED
on(addUser, (state, { user }) => {
  state.users.push(user);   // same array object
  return state;             // same state object
});
// OnPush sees oldRef === newRef -> "nothing changed" -> NO re-render.
// The data updated. The screen didn't. Classic "why isn't my UI updating?"

// ✔ new references all the way up
on(addUser, (state, { user }) => ({ ...state, users: [...state.users, user] }));

NgRx selectors, distinctUntilChanged, and OnPush all compare by reference (===). Mutation defeats every one of them at once. That's the whole reason the rule exists — it's not dogma, it's what makes the fast path work.

Spread is shallow — the trap

// ✘ looks immutable, isn't — the nested object is still shared
{ ...state, user: { ...state.user } }   // state.user.address is the SAME object

// ✔ every level you change needs a new reference
{ ...state, user: { ...state.user, address: { ...state.user.address, city } } }

This is exactly why deep state trees are painful and why @ngrx/entity (flat, normalised) exists.

Let the framework catch you

StoreModule.forRoot(reducers, {
  runtimeChecks: {
    strictStateImmutability: true,   // throws if you mutate state
    strictActionImmutability: true
  }
});

These freeze state in dev, so a mutation throws immediately instead of becoming a "why won't my list refresh?" mystery. On by default in modern NgRx.

How to say it in an interview

“Purity is what makes time-travel work — DevTools replays actions from the initial state, so a Date.now() or an HTTP call in a reducer makes the replay diverge. Immutability is about change detection: selectors and OnPush compare by reference, so mutating an array means the reference is unchanged and the UI never re-renders even though the data did. Spread is shallow, which is the usual trap — and I turn on strictStateImmutability so it throws instead of failing silently.”

question: What are selectors and why are they memoised?
answer: Selectors are pure functions that read and derive data from the store. createSelector memoises the result — it recomputes only when its inputs change, avoiding needless recalculation and re-renders.
tip: Compose selectors from smaller ones for reuse and better memoization.
deep:
What memoisation actually does

export const selectActiveUsers = createSelector(
  selectUsers,                          // input selector
  (users) => users.filter(u => u.active) // projector — the expensive bit
);

createSelector caches the last input references and the last result. On each store emission it compares the new inputs by ===:

  • Inputs unchanged → return the cached result object, skip the projector entirely.
  • Any input changed → re-run the projector, cache the new result.

Two payoffs. The projector doesn't re-run (saves CPU on filter/sort/map), and — more importantly — the returned reference is stable, so OnPush components don't re-render.

Why the reference matters more than the CPU

// ✘ no selector — a NEW array every single store emission
users$ = this.store.select(s => s.users.filter(u => u.active));
// Any unrelated state change (a toast, a spinner) creates a new array ref
// -> every OnPush component using it re-renders. Death by a thousand cuts.

Memoisation cache size is 1. That's enough because the store emits sequentially, but it's why alternating inputs thrash the cache.

Compose — don't write fat selectors

const selectUserState = createFeatureSelector<UserState>('users');
const selectUsers     = createSelector(selectUserState, s => s.users);
const selectFilter    = createSelector(selectUserState, s => s.filter);

// each layer memoises independently
export const selectVisibleUsers = createSelector(
  selectUsers, selectFilter,
  (users, filter) => users.filter(u => u.name.includes(filter))
);

If filter changes but users doesn't, only the final projector re-runs — the upstream ones return cached values.

Parameterised selectors — the known trap

// ✘ factory called per component -> a NEW selector each time -> NO shared cache
export const selectUserById = (id: string) =>
  createSelector(selectUsers, users => users.find(u => u.id === id));

// ✔ props-style — one selector instance, cache preserved
export const selectUserById = createSelector(
  selectUsers,
  (users: User[], props: { id: string }) => users.find(u => u.id === props.id)
);
this.store.select(selectUserById, { id });

Even the props form only caches the last id — two components with different ids thrash it. For per-id lookups, @ngrx/entity's entities[id] dictionary is the right answer: O(1), no cache thrash.

Derive, never store

Don't put activeUserCount in state and update it in three reducers — it will go stale. Store the minimum; derive the rest in selectors. "Can I compute this from what's already in the store?" → then it's a selector.

How to say it in an interview

“Selectors are pure queries, and createSelector memoises on its input references — if they're unchanged it skips the projector and returns the same result reference, which is what stops OnPush components re-rendering on unrelated state changes. I compose small selectors so each layer memoises independently, and I keep derived data out of state entirely. The trap is parameterised selectors — a factory per component gets you a fresh cache each time, so for by-id lookups I'd use @ngrx/entity's dictionary.”

question: What are Effects and why not call APIs in reducers?
answer: Effects isolate side effects (HTTP, routing, storage) from reducers. An effect listens to an action stream, runs async work, and maps it to a new action. Reducers must stay pure, so any I/O belongs in effects.
tip: Use switchMap in effects for cancel-on-new (loads) and concatMap for ordered writes.
deep:
What an effect is

An effect is an observable of actions, in and out. It listens to the global action stream, filters with ofType, does something impure, and (usually) maps the result to a new action that a reducer can fold in.

loadUsers$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(loadUsers),                       // 1. filter the stream
  switchMap(() => this.api.getUsers().pipe( // 2. side effect
    map(users => loadUsersSuccess({ users })),      // 3. success action
    catchError(err => of(loadUsersFailure({ err }))) // 4. failure action
  ))
));

this.actions$ is a hot stream of every action dispatched anywhere in the app. Effects and reducers both receive each action — reducers first (sync), then effects.

Why not call the API in a reducer?

Reducers must be pure so DevTools can replay actions to reconstruct state. An HTTP call in a reducer would fire again on every replay, and would return different data each time — time-travel breaks, tests need mocks, and the reducer is no longer a function you can reason about. So all impurity is pushed to the edge: effects.

The error rule — this is the one that bites

// ✘ BROKEN — catchError on the OUTER stream
loadUsers$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(loadUsers),
  switchMap(() => this.api.getUsers()),
  map(users => loadUsersSuccess({ users })),
  catchError(err => of(loadUsersFailure({ err })))   // outer!
));

One failed request errors the outer stream. catchError handles it — and then the outer observable completes. The effect is now dead for the rest of the app's lifetime: dispatch loadUsers again and nothing happens, with no error in the console. Fix: catchError inside the inner pipe, so the outer action stream never sees an error. This is the single most common NgRx bug.

Choosing the flattening operator matters here

switchMap for loads/search (cancel stale), concatMap for ordered writes, exhaustMap for login/submit (ignore double-clicks), mergeMap for independent parallel work. Using switchMap on a save is a real bug — a second save cancels the first, and the user silently loses data.

Effects that dispatch nothing

navigate$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(loginSuccess),
  tap(() => this.router.navigate(['/dashboard']))
), { dispatch: false });   // required — or NgRx re-dispatches the action -> infinite loop

Modern: functional effects

export const loadUsers$ = createEffect(
  (actions$ = inject(Actions), api = inject(ApiService)) =>
    actions$.pipe(ofType(loadUsers), switchMap(() => ...)),
  { functional: true }
);

How to say it in an interview

“An effect takes actions in and returns actions out — it's where everything impure lives, because reducers have to stay pure for replay and testing. The critical detail is catchError placement: on the outer stream it handles the first error and then the effect completes permanently, so the feature silently stops working. It goes inside the inner observable, returning a failure action. And the flattening operator is a real decision — switchMap on a save would cancel an in-flight write.”

question: NgRx store vs a service with BehaviorSubject.
answer: A service + BehaviorSubject is simple shared state — great for small/medium apps. NgRx adds structure: strict unidirectional flow, DevTools time-travel, effects, and clear conventions that scale to large teams — at the cost of boilerplate.
tip: Signals-based stores (NgRx SignalStore, or a plain signal service) are the modern lightweight middle ground.
deep:
A BehaviorSubject service is a store

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class CartService {
  private state$ = new BehaviorSubject<CartState>(initial);

  readonly items$ = this.state$.pipe(map(s => s.items), distinctUntilChanged());

  add(item: Item) {
    const s = this.state$.value;
    this.state$.next({ ...s, items: [...s.items, item] });   // immutable update
  }
}

That's ~10 lines and it covers a large share of what people use NgRx for. Being able to write it is what makes “when would you not use NgRx?” credible.

What NgRx adds — and it's not features

Service + BehaviorSubjectNgRx Store
Boilerplateminimal3 actions + reducer + effect + selector
Who can change stateanyone who injects itonly via a dispatched action
DevTools / time-travelnoyes
Change audit trailnoevery action logged
Memoised derivationhand-rolledcreateSelector
Async patternsad hoc per serviceeffects, one convention
Learning curvelowreal

The row that matters is "who can change state". In a service, any component can call .next() or even state$.value and mutate. With 5 teams, state changes from 40 places and nobody can answer "why did this become null?". NgRx's constraint — the only way to change state is to dispatch a serialisable action — is the actual product. Everything else follows from it.

Where the service approach breaks down

  • Debuggability at scale — no log of what changed and why.
  • Discipline drift — someone exposes the Subject publicly, or mutates .value directly, and the invariant is gone with no lint to catch it.
  • Convention drift — 10 services, 10 different shapes. New joiners learn each one.

Note the first bullet is fixable: you can wire up your own devtools. The second and third are social problems — which is precisely why NgRx pays off with team size, not app size.

The modern middle ground: SignalStore

export const CartStore = signalStore(
  { providedIn: 'root' },
  withState(initial),
  withComputed(({ items }) => ({ total: computed(() => items().length) })),
  withMethods((store, api = inject(ApiService)) => ({
    add(item: Item) { patchState(store, s => ({ items: [...s.items, item] })); }
  }))
);

Structure and conventions of NgRx, no actions/reducers/effects boilerplate, signals-native. In 2026 this is often the right default — mention it.

How to say it in an interview

“A BehaviorSubject service is a store — I can write one in ten lines, and for most apps it's enough. What NgRx really adds isn't features, it's a constraint: state can only change by dispatching a serialisable action. That's what gives you the DevTools log and time-travel, and it's what stops forty call sites mutating state directly once you have several teams. So the deciding factor is team size and debuggability, not app size. These days I'd look at SignalStore first — most of the structure, far less ceremony.”

question: What is @ngrx/entity?
answer: A helper that stores collections in a normalised { ids: [], entities: {} } shape and provides an adapter with CRUD helpers (addOne, updateOne, removeOne) plus ready-made selectors — removing hand-written immutable array logic.
tip: Normalising by id makes lookups O(1) and updates immutable-friendly.
code:
const adapter = createEntityAdapter<User>();
const initialState = adapter.getInitialState();
deep:
The problem it solves

// Array state — every update is a hand-written immutable crawl
on(updateUser, (state, { user }) => ({
  ...state,
  users: state.users.map(u => u.id === user.id ? { ...u, ...user } : u)  // O(n)
}));
on(removeUser, (state, { id }) => ({
  ...state,
  users: state.users.filter(u => u.id !== id)   // O(n), easy to get wrong
}));

Every collection reducer becomes a map/filter/findIndex puzzle. Lookups are O(n). And map returns a new array reference even when nothing matched — so every OnPush list re-renders on a no-op update.

The normalised shape

{
  ids: ['u1', 'u3', 'u2'],           // order lives here
  entities: {                        // lookup lives here — O(1)
    u1: { id: 'u1', name: 'Ann' },
    u2: { id: 'u2', name: 'Bob' },
    u3: { id: 'u3', name: 'Cat' }
  }
}

This is the same idea as a database index: separate identity from ordering. It's the standard Redux normalisation pattern, and @ngrx/entity just implements it for you.

The adapter

export const adapter = createEntityAdapter<User>({
  selectId: (u) => u.userId,                       // if not 'id'
  sortComparer: (a, b) => a.name.localeCompare(b.name)  // keeps ids[] sorted
});

export const initialState = adapter.getInitialState({ loading: false });

export const reducer = createReducer(
  initialState,
  on(loadSuccess, (state, { users }) => adapter.setAll(users, state)),
  on(addUser,     (state, { user })  => adapter.addOne(user, state)),
  on(updateUser,  (state, { user })  => adapter.updateOne({ id: user.id, changes: user }, state)),
  on(removeUser,  (state, { id })    => adapter.removeOne(id, state))
);

Each helper returns a correctly-updated immutable state — no spread gymnastics.

Free selectors

const { selectAll, selectEntities, selectIds, selectTotal } = adapter.getSelectors();

export const selectUserState = createFeatureSelector<State>('users');
export const selectAllUsers  = createSelector(selectUserState, selectAll);      // User[]
export const selectUserMap   = createSelector(selectUserState, selectEntities); // dictionary
export const selectUserCount = createSelector(selectUserState, selectTotal);

selectEntities is the good answer to parameterised selectors: selectUserMap()[id] is an O(1) lookup with no memoisation cache to thrash.

setAll vs addMany vs upsertMany

MethodBehaviour
setAllreplaces the whole collection
addManyadds; ignores existing ids
upsertManyadds new, updates existing ← usually what you want for paging
updateOnepartial changes; no-op if id missing

addOne silently doing nothing when the id already exists is a classic "why isn't my item updating?" — use upsertOne.

How to say it in an interview

“It normalises a collection into ids plus an entities dictionary, so lookups are O(1) instead of a find, and the adapter gives you immutable CRUD helpers so reducers aren't hand-written map/filter chains. You also get selectAll/selectEntities for free — and selectEntities is how I do by-id lookups without thrashing a memoised selector's cache. The gotcha is addOne silently ignoring an existing id; upsertOne is usually what you actually want.”

question: What do the Redux DevTools give you with NgRx?
answer: A visual log of every action, the state before/after, and time-travel debugging — you can replay actions and jump to any past state. This traceability is a core reason to adopt NgRx on large apps.
tip: Mention time-travel debugging — it's the standout benefit interviewers look for.
code: StoreDevtoolsModule.instrument({ maxAge: 25 })

question: What's a good convention for naming actions?
answer: Use the pattern [Source] Event — describe what happened, not what to do. Group by feature and event (e.g. [Auth API] Login Success). Prefer many specific, descriptive actions over a few generic setters.
tip: Actions are events, not commands — name them in the past tense.

question: What are Actions in NgRx? How do you create them using createAction()?
answer: Actions are plain objects that describe what happened in the application. They are the only way to change the store's state. Modern NgRx uses createAction() to define actions, optionally with props() to carry data.
tip: Remember: Actions describe events, not commands. Use names like [User API] Load Success instead of SetUsers.

question: What is props() in NgRx?
answer: props() defines the payload carried by an action. Instead of manually creating an object, it provides strongly typed data that reducers and effects can safely access.
tip: Think of props() as the parameters passed along with an action.

question: Why is createReducer() preferred over the old switch statement?
answer: createReducer() is more readable, type-safe and easier to maintain. It avoids long switch statements and works seamlessly with on() handlers. It also provides better TypeScript support and cleaner code.
tip: Modern NgRx applications should always prefer createReducer().

question: What is the on() function inside createReducer()?
answer: on() connects one or more actions to a reducer function. Whenever one of those actions is dispatched, the reducer executes and returns a new immutable state.
tip: Think of on() as the replacement for each case inside a switch statement.

question: When should you use switchMap(), mergeMap(), concatMap() and exhaustMap() inside NgRx Effects?
answer:
Each RxJS mapping operator has a different behavior:

  • switchMap() — Cancels the previous request and keeps only the latest one. Best for Search or Auto Complete.
  • mergeMap() — Runs all requests in parallel. Best when every request must complete independently.
  • concatMap() — Executes requests one after another in order. Best for sequential updates.
  • exhaustMap() — Ignores new requests while one is already running. Best for Login or Submit buttons to prevent duplicate requests.
tip: Interviewers almost always ask which operator you'd use for Search, Login and Save APIs.
deep:
The decision is about concurrency

All four take an action and return an inner observable. They differ only in what happens when a new action arrives while an inner request is still in flight.

OperatorNew action arrives mid-flightUse forWrong choice costs you
switchMapcancels the previoussearch, autocomplete, loadcancels a save → data loss
mergeMapruns both in parallelindependent work (uploads)out-of-order results
concatMapqueues it, in orderordered writes, sequential savesslow if requests are long
exhaustMapignores itlogin, submitdrops legitimate retries

The three the interviewer will name

// SEARCH -> switchMap. Stale results must never overwrite fresh ones.
search$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(search),
  switchMap(({ term }) => this.api.search(term).pipe(
    map(r => searchSuccess({ r })),
    catchError(e => of(searchFailure({ e })))
  ))
));

// LOGIN -> exhaustMap. User double-clicks; ignore the second click entirely.
login$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(login),
  exhaustMap(({ creds }) => this.api.login(creds).pipe(...))
));

// SAVE -> concatMap. Every write must happen, in order.
save$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(save),
  concatMap(({ item }) => this.api.save(item).pipe(...))
));

Why switchMap on a save is a genuine bug

The user edits row 1, hits save, immediately edits row 2 and hits save. switchMap cancels the first request — row 1 is never persisted, and the UI happily shows success. Silent data loss that only reproduces when the user is fast. Writes want concatMap; reads want switchMap.

exhaustMap vs a disabled button

Disabling the button is UI-level and can be bypassed (double-click before the flag flips, Enter key, a script). exhaustMap enforces it at the stream level: while a login is in flight, further login actions are dropped. Defence in depth — do both.

The rule of thumb

  • Read (GET, only latest matters) → switchMap
  • Write (POST/PUT, all must land in order) → concatMap
  • Idempotent + independent → mergeMap
  • Must not double-fire → exhaustMap

The catchError placement still applies

Whichever you pick, catchError goes on the inner observable. On the outer, the first failure completes the effect permanently.

How to say it in an interview

“They're four concurrency strategies for the same shape. Search gets switchMap so a slow stale response can't overwrite the latest. Login gets exhaustMap so a double-click is ignored at the stream level, not just by disabling a button. Saves get concatMap so every write lands in order — using switchMap there would cancel an in-flight save and silently lose the user's data. mergeMap when the requests are genuinely independent. Reads cancel, writes queue.”

question: How do you handle API Success and Failure in NgRx?
answer: Follow a three-action pattern: Load, Load Success, and Load Failure. The component dispatches the Load action, the Effect calls the API, then dispatches either Success or Failure. The Reducer updates the store accordingly.
tip: Almost every API call in NgRx follows the pattern: Request → Success → Failure.
deep:
Why three actions, not one

An async call has three distinct moments, and each needs different state:

loadUsers        -> loading: true,  error: null
loadUsersSuccess -> loading: false, users: [...]
loadUsersFailure -> loading: false, error: '...'

Squashing them into one action means you can't render a spinner or an error — the reducer has no way to know which phase you're in. The triplet is why the pattern is universal.

The full slice

// actions — note the SOURCE in the name
export const loadUsers        = createAction('[Users Page] Load');
export const loadUsersSuccess = createAction('[Users API] Load Success',
  props<{ users: User[] }>());
export const loadUsersFailure = createAction('[Users API] Load Failure',
  props<{ error: string }>());

// reducer — all three phases
export const reducer = createReducer(
  initialState,
  on(loadUsers,        (s) => ({ ...s, loading: true, error: null })),
  on(loadUsersSuccess, (s, { users }) => ({ ...s, loading: false, users })),
  on(loadUsersFailure, (s, { error }) => ({ ...s, loading: false, error }))
);

// effect — catchError INSIDE
loadUsers$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(loadUsers),
  switchMap(() => this.api.getUsers().pipe(
    map(users => loadUsersSuccess({ users })),
    catchError(err => of(loadUsersFailure({ error: err.message })))
  ))
));

Naming: '[Source] Event', not '[Feature] Command'

Note [Users Page] vs [Users API] — the source differs. That's deliberate: in DevTools you instantly see whether a load came from the page, a resolver, or a refresh poll. Reusing one '[Users] Load' for all three destroys that. Actions are cheap; be specific.

Why failure must be an action, not a thrown error

  • It's serialisable — DevTools logs it and time-travel replays it.
  • The reducer can render an error state.
  • Other effects can react to it (log to Sentry, show a toast) without coupling.

The bug this pattern hides

// ✘ catchError outside — first failure kills the effect FOREVER
switchMap(() => this.api.getUsers()),
map(users => loadUsersSuccess({ users })),
catchError(err => of(loadUsersFailure({ error: err })))

The error propagates to the outer actions$ stream, catchError handles it once, and the outer observable completes. Retry the load and nothing happens — no console error, no clue. Inner placement is non-negotiable.

Storing the error

Store a string or a small serialisable object, never the raw HttpErrorResponse — it's a class instance and trips strictStateSerializability, and it can't be replayed.

How to say it in an interview

“Request/Success/Failure, because the three phases need different state — loading, data, error — and one action can't express that. The effect maps the API result to a success or failure action, always with catchError inside the inner pipe: outside, the first failure completes the effect permanently and the feature silently stops responding. I name actions by source — [Users Page] Load vs [Users API] Load Success — so DevTools tells me where it came from, and I store a serialisable error string rather than the HttpErrorResponse.”

question: What is the difference between the Root Store and a Feature Store?
answer: The Root Store contains global application state (authentication, user, settings). A Feature Store contains state for a specific feature or module (Products, Orders, Dashboard). Feature stores are registered independently and keep the application modular.
tip: Global state belongs in the Root Store. Feature-specific state belongs in a Feature Store.
code:
StoreModule.forRoot({ auth: authReducer });

StoreModule.forFeature('products', productReducer);
deep:
Root vs feature

Root StoreFeature Store
Registered withprovideStore() / forRoot()provideState() / forFeature()
Registeredonce, at bootstrapper feature, often lazily
Holdsauth, current user, settingsproducts, orders, dashboard
Liveswhole app lifetimefrom first load onward
// root
bootstrapApplication(App, {
  providers: [provideStore({ auth: authReducer }), provideEffects([AuthEffects])]
});

// lazy feature route — registered when the route is first visited
{
  path: 'orders',
  providers: [provideState(ordersFeature), provideEffects([OrdersEffects])],
  loadChildren: () => import('./orders/routes')
}

The bit that surprises people: features are dynamic

The store's state tree changes shape at runtime. Before you visit /orders:

{ auth: {...} }
// after visiting /orders:
{ auth: {...}, orders: {...} }

This has three real consequences:

  • A root-level selector reading state.orders gets undefined until the feature loads — hence createFeatureSelector, which is written to tolerate it.
  • A feature's reducer never sees actions dispatched before it registered. Dispatch loadOrders from a root component before /orders is loaded and nothing happens.
  • Feature state is not removed when you navigate away — it stays in the tree. It's lazily added, not scoped.

createFeature — the modern way

export const ordersFeature = createFeature({
  name: 'orders',
  reducer: createReducer(initialState, on(...)),
});
// gives you selectOrdersState + a selector per state property, for free
export const { selectOrdersState, selectItems, selectLoading } = ordersFeature;

This removes most hand-written createFeatureSelector/createSelector boilerplate. Mentioning it signals you're current.

Cross-feature access

Feature A can select from feature B's state — but it couples them, and it breaks if B hasn't loaded. Prefer: put genuinely shared state in root, or have A react to B's actions in an effect rather than reading its state.

The naming trap

createFeatureSelector<OrderState>('orders')   // string must match forFeature('orders')

A typo compiles fine and yields undefined at runtime. createFeature removes the stringly-typed link entirely.

How to say it in an interview

“Root state is registered at bootstrap and holds global things like auth; feature state is registered per feature, usually in a lazy route, so it's added to the state tree only when that feature loads. The consequence worth knowing is that the tree's shape is dynamic — a feature reducer doesn't exist yet, so it won't see actions dispatched before it registers, and feature state isn't torn down when you navigate away. I use createFeature now, which generates the selectors and kills the stringly-typed createFeatureSelector name.”

question: How does NgRx work with Lazy Loaded Modules?
answer: Each lazy-loaded module can register its own reducer and effects using StoreModule.forFeature() and EffectsModule.forFeature(). The feature state is loaded only when the module is visited, improving startup performance.
tip: Use forFeature() inside lazy modules instead of forRoot().

question: What is createFeatureSelector() and why is it used?
answer: createFeatureSelector() selects an entire feature state from the store. Other selectors are then composed from it using createSelector(). This improves code organization and reusability.
tip: Always create one Feature Selector first, then derive all other selectors from it.

question: What is the difference between createFeatureSelector() and createSelector()?
answer: createFeatureSelector() selects an entire feature slice from the store. createSelector() derives or computes smaller pieces of data from one or more selectors. In practice, you usually create one feature selector first, then build multiple selectors on top of it.
tip: Feature Selector gets the feature. Selector gets the data inside the feature.

question: How do you reset the NgRx Store when a user logs out?
answer: Dispatch a logout action and reset the state inside the root reducer (or a meta reducer) by returning the initialState. This clears all user-specific data from the store.
tip: Clearing the store on logout prevents the next user from seeing stale data.

question: How do you persist the NgRx Store after a page refresh?
answer: NgRx Store lives in memory, so refreshing the page clears it. To persist state, save selected store slices to localStorage (or sessionStorage) using a Meta Reducer or libraries like ngrx-store-localstorage, then restore them when the application starts.
tip: Persist only required data like user preferences or auth information—not the entire store.
deep:
Why it's needed

The store is in memory. F5 and it's gone — back to initialState. Persistence means writing chosen slices out and rehydrating them at startup.

The meta-reducer approach

export function persistState(reducer: ActionReducer<State>): ActionReducer<State> {
  return (state, action) => {
    // rehydrate once, on init
    if (action.type === INIT || action.type === UPDATE) {
      const saved = localStorage.getItem('app-state');
      if (saved) {
        try { return { ...reducer(state, action), ...JSON.parse(saved) }; }
        catch { localStorage.removeItem('app-state'); }   // corrupt -> drop it
      }
    }
    const next = reducer(state, action);
    // persist only what you need
    localStorage.setItem('app-state', JSON.stringify({ prefs: next.prefs }));
    return next;
  };
}

Or use ngrx-store-localstorage, which is this with the edge cases handled.

Persist a whitelist, never the whole store

PersistNever persist
UI prefs (theme, language)Auth tokens — XSS reads localStorage
Draft form contentCached server data — goes stale instantly
Cart (sometimes)loading / error flags
Onboarding progressAnything sensitive/PII

Two answers that earn credit:

  • Tokens don't belong in localStorage — any XSS can read it. Prefer an httpOnly cookie. If asked "how do you persist auth?", say this.
  • Persisting loading: true is the classic self-inflicted bug: the user refreshes mid-request and the app rehydrates into a permanent spinner, because no request is in flight to ever set it false.

The migration problem nobody thinks about

// v1 shipped:  { user: { name } }
// v2 expects:  { user: { firstName, lastName } }
// A returning user rehydrates v1 data into v2 code -> undefined everywhere.

Persisted state is data you now have to version, exactly like a database schema. Store a version number and either migrate or discard on mismatch. Wrap rehydration in try/catch — hand-edited or corrupt JSON must not brick the app.

Performance

localStorage is synchronous and blocks the main thread; JSON.stringify of a large tree on every action is a real jank source. Persist a narrow slice, and debounce if it's hot.

How to say it in an interview

“A meta reducer that rehydrates on INIT and writes selected slices back after each action — or ngrx-store-localstorage. The important part is what you persist: a whitelist like theme or a draft, never the whole store. Not cached server data, because it's stale on arrival; not loading, or a refresh mid-request rehydrates into a permanent spinner; and not auth tokens, because any XSS can read localStorage — that's an httpOnly cookie. And persisted state needs versioning, since old shapes will rehydrate into new code.”

question: What are Meta Reducers in NgRx?
answer: Meta Reducers are higher-order reducers that wrap normal reducers. They execute before or after every reducer call and are commonly used for logging, state persistence, resetting state, or debugging.
tip: Think of Meta Reducers as middleware for reducers.
deep:
What they are

A meta reducer is a higher-order reducer: it takes a reducer and returns a reducer. That lets it wrap every action for every slice — Redux middleware, in NgRx terms.

export function logger(reducer: ActionReducer<State>): ActionReducer<State> {
  return (state, action) => {
    const next = reducer(state, action);   // run the real reducer
    console.groupCollapsed(action.type);
    console.log('prev', state); console.log('next', next);
    console.groupEnd();
    return next;
  };
}

provideStore(reducers, { metaReducers: [logger] });

You can act before the inner reducer (inspect/replace the action or state) or after (inspect/transform the result) — the position of your code around the reducer(state, action) call is the hook.

The killer use: reset state on logout

export function clearState(reducer: ActionReducer<State>): ActionReducer<State> {
  return (state, action) => {
    if (action.type === logout.type) {
      state = undefined;          // every reducer re-initialises
    }
    return reducer(state, action);
  };
}

Passing undefined makes every reducer fall back to its initialState — one function wipes the entire tree. The alternative is an on(logout, () => initialState) in all 20 reducers, and you will forget one, leaking the previous user's data into the next session. This is the answer that makes the concept click.

Other real uses

  • Persistence — rehydrate from localStorage on INIT, write back after each action (this is how ngrx-store-localstorage works).
  • Freeze — throw on mutation in dev (NgRx's own strictStateImmutability runtime check is a built-in meta reducer).
  • Undo/redo — keep a stack of past states.
  • Telemetry — ship action types to analytics.

Order matters

metaReducers: [logger, clearState]
// composed right-to-left: logger(clearState(reducer))
// -> clearState runs closest to the real reducer; logger sees the final result

Keep them pure — mostly

A meta reducer sits in the reducer path, so the purity rules apply: no HTTP, no routing. Logging and localStorage writes are the pragmatic exceptions everyone makes (and are technically impure) — anything more, and it belongs in an effect.

Dev-only

export const metaReducers: MetaReducer<State>[] = isDevMode() ? [logger] : [];

How to say it in an interview

“They're higher-order reducers — reducer in, reducer out — so they intercept every action across every slice, which is basically Redux middleware. The case I always reach for is logout: a meta reducer that sets state to undefined on the logout action makes every reducer fall back to its initial state, so one function clears the whole tree instead of adding a reset handler to twenty reducers and forgetting one. They also power persistence and NgRx's own immutability runtime checks.”

question: What are Runtime Checks in NgRx?
answer: Runtime Checks help detect common mistakes such as mutating state, mutating actions, or storing non-serializable objects. They improve application reliability during development by throwing errors when NgRx best practices are violated.
tip: Enable Runtime Checks in development to catch accidental state mutations early.

question: What is the Facade Pattern in NgRx and why is it used?
answer: A Facade is an Angular service that hides NgRx implementation details from components. Components interact only with the Facade instead of directly dispatching actions or selecting state. This reduces coupling, simplifies testing, and makes it easier to replace NgRx later if needed.
tip: Components should know about business operations, not NgRx APIs.
deep:
What it looks like

@Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' })
export class UserFacade {
  private store = inject(Store);

  // state — components see plain streams, not selectors
  readonly users$   = this.store.select(selectUsers);
  readonly loading$ = this.store.select(selectLoading);

  // intent — components call business operations, not dispatch()
  loadUsers()            { this.store.dispatch(loadUsers()); }
  addUser(user: User)    { this.store.dispatch(addUser({ user })); }
}
// component — no NgRx imports at all
export class UserListComponent {
  private facade = inject(UserFacade);
  users$ = this.facade.users$;
  ngOnInit() { this.facade.loadUsers(); }
}

What you gain

  • Decoupling — components import zero NgRx. Swapping NgRx for SignalStore, or a plain service, touches the facade only.
  • Testing — mock one facade instead of provideMockStore plus selectors plus action assertions.
  • Discoverability — UserFacade is a readable API surface for the feature. New joiners don't need to grep actions.
  • Intent over mechanics — facade.addUser(u) reads better than store.dispatch(addUser({ user: u })).

The criticism — and you should voice it

This is contested, and knowing that is the senior signal. The NgRx maintainers have argued against blanket facades. The objections:

  • It hides the flow. NgRx's value is explicit, traceable data flow; a facade re-introduces an opaque indirection layer over it.
  • It's another layer to maintain — often a mechanical 1:1 passthrough that just adds a file.
  • It can become a god object. One AppFacade with 40 methods is worse than the thing it wrapped.
  • It encourages calling several methods in sequence, which recreates imperative orchestration in components — the exact thing actions were meant to prevent.

The "swap NgRx out later" argument is also mostly theoretical — almost nobody does.

The balanced position

Use a facade when it adds something: a large feature with many selectors/actions, a library boundary where you don't want to leak NgRx, or a team that benefits from the smaller API surface. Skip it when it's a passthrough — store.select() in the component is fine and more honest.

How to say it in an interview

“A facade is a service that hides Store, actions and selectors behind a feature-level API, so components call facade.loadUsers() and never import NgRx. It's nice for testing and for keeping NgRx out of a library's public surface. But it's genuinely debated — the maintainers push back, because it adds an indirection layer over a pattern whose main selling point is explicit traceable flow, and it often ends up a 1:1 passthrough or a god object. I'd use one on a big feature or at a library boundary, not reflexively.”

question: Should Angular components access the Store directly?
answer: Yes, but only for simple applications. In medium or large projects, components should communicate through a Facade Service instead of directly using the Store. This reduces coupling, improves testability, and makes it easier to change the state management implementation later.
tip: Large enterprise applications usually place a Facade between the Component and the Store.

question: Should a component dispatch multiple NgRx actions?
answer: Generally no. Components should dispatch a single business action such as loadUsers(). If multiple actions are needed, they should be dispatched from an Effect. This keeps components simple and business logic centralized.
tip: Keep components 'dumb' and move orchestration into Effects.

question: What is the difference between Smart and Dumb Components in NgRx?
answer: Smart Components interact with the Store (or Facade), dispatch actions, and manage business logic. Dumb Components receive data through @Input() and emit events using @Output(). They contain little or no business logic and are highly reusable.
tip: Keep presentation components reusable by avoiding Store dependencies.
code:
// Smart Component
<app-user-list
  [users]="users$ | async"
  (delete)="deleteUser($event)">
</app-user-list>

question: When should you use NgRx Store instead of Component State?
answer: Use Component State for local UI state like dialog visibility, selected tab, or input values. Use the NgRx Store for shared, long-lived application state such as logged-in user, products, orders, or shopping cart data.
tip: Ask yourself: 'Does another feature need this data?' If yes, it probably belongs in the Store.
deep:
The test

Ask two questions:

  1. Does anything outside this component need it? No → component state.
  2. Should it survive this component being destroyed? No → component state.

If both are no, putting it in the store is strictly worse: more code, more indirection, and a global variable pretending to be architecture.

Where things belong

StateHomeWhy
Dialog open, active tab, hovercomponentnothing else cares
Form input while typingcomponent / formstore dispatch per keystroke is madness
Table sort/page (not shared)componentdies with the view
Table sort/page (shareable URL)URLsee below
Logged-in user, permissionsstoreeverything reads it
Shopping cartstoreheader badge + cart page + checkout
Cached server liststore or a servicedepends on sharing

The third option nobody mentions: the URL

/orders?page=2&sort=date&filter=open

Filters, pagination, selected tab, search terms — these are often router state, not store state. Put them in the URL and you get shareable links, working back/forward, and refresh-survival for free. Volunteering this is a strong signal, because it shows you don't reach for the store reflexively.

Symptoms of over-storing

  • setDialogOpen / setActiveTab actions cluttering the DevTools log so the real events are unfindable.
  • State that must be reset on every component destroy — you've built a leak, and now you need cleanup actions.
  • Two instances of the same component fighting over one store slice (a modal opened twice, a list rendered in two panels).

That last one is the killer argument: global state can't be instantiated twice. Component state naturally can.

The middle rung: ComponentStore / SignalStore

// feature-scoped, dies with the component — provided at component level
@Component({ providers: [ProductsStore] })

For state that's too complex for a field but shouldn't be global — a multi-step wizard, a complex filter panel — ComponentStore or a component-provided SignalStore gives you structure with the right lifetime. That's the answer to "what if it's complex but local?"

How to say it in an interview

“I ask whether anything outside the component needs it, and whether it should outlive the component. Both no — it's component state; isDialogOpen in the global store is just a global variable with extra steps, and it breaks the moment the component renders twice. The option people forget is the URL: filters and pagination usually belong there, so links are shareable and refresh works. If it's complex but still local, I'd use a component-provided SignalStore rather than promoting it to global.”

question: How do multiple Reducers work in an NgRx application?
answer: Each feature has its own reducer responsible for managing only its own slice of state. NgRx combines all reducers into a single Root Store. This keeps features independent, modular, and easier to maintain.
tip: Think of each reducer as managing one feature, not the whole application.

question: What is the difference between Optimistic Updates and Pessimistic Updates in NgRx?
answer: Optimistic Update updates the UI immediately before the API responds, assuming success. If the API fails, the state is rolled back. Pessimistic Update waits for the API response before updating the Store, ensuring consistency but with a slower UI experience.
tip: Optimistic = Faster UI. Pessimistic = Safer data.
deep:
The two flows

PESSIMISTIC  (safe, slower)
dispatch(save) -> effect -> API ---wait--- 200 -> dispatch(saveSuccess) -> reducer -> UI
                                       \--- 500 -> dispatch(saveFailure) -> show error

OPTIMISTIC   (fast, riskier)
dispatch(save) -> reducer updates UI IMMEDIATELY
              \-> effect -> API ---- 200 -> (nothing to do)
                                 \--- 500 -> dispatch(saveRollback) -> undo it

Optimistic in practice — you must keep the old value

// reducer — apply immediately, stash the previous for rollback
on(toggleLike, (state, { id }) => adapter.updateOne(
  { id, changes: { liked: true } }, state
));
on(toggleLikeFailure, (state, { id, previous }) => adapter.updateOne(
  { id, changes: { liked: previous } }, state          // roll back
));

// effect — fire and forget, but map failure to a rollback carrying the old value
toggleLike$ = createEffect(() => this.actions$.pipe(
  ofType(toggleLike),
  concatLatestFrom(({ id }) => this.store.select(selectLiked(id))),
  mergeMap(([{ id }, previous]) => this.api.like(id).pipe(
    map(() => toggleLikeSuccess({ id })),
    catchError(() => of(toggleLikeFailure({ id, previous })))   // carry it back
  ))
));

The rollback action must carry the previous value. That's the part candidates miss — "roll back" isn't magic; you need the old state, captured before the change.

When to use which

OptimisticPessimistic
High success rateFailure is likely/costly
Cheap to undoServer assigns data (id, total)
Reversible & low-stakesMoney, orders, legal records
Like, star, reorder, mark-read, delete-with-undoCheckout, payment, submit form

The test: if this fails and we silently undo it, how bad is that? A like flicking back is fine. A bank transfer appearing then vanishing is not.

Why optimistic is tempting and dangerous

  • UI feels instant — no spinner, no latency. That's the whole point.
  • But rollback is confusing UX if the user already moved on — the list reorders itself 3 seconds later.
  • Concurrent edits make it much harder: two optimistic updates in flight, one fails — rolling back to "previous" can clobber the other's result.
  • Server-generated values can't be faked: you don't know the real id or createdAt, so a temp id needs reconciling on success.

Note the operator

Optimistic updates usually want mergeMap or concatMap, not switchMap — cancelling the request would leave the UI showing a change that was never persisted.

How to say it in an interview

“Pessimistic waits for the API and only then updates the store — safe, but the user stares at a spinner. Optimistic updates state immediately and rolls back on failure, so it feels instant. The implementation detail people miss is that the failure action has to carry the previous value, since you need it to undo. I use optimistic for cheap reversible things like a like or a reorder, and pessimistic for anything where a silent undo would be alarming — payments, orders. And not switchMap, or you'd cancel the write and leave the UI lying.”

question: What is @ngrx/router-store and why is it used?
answer: @ngrx/router-store synchronizes Angular Router state with the NgRx Store. It allows selectors, reducers, and effects to access the current route, query parameters, and navigation events without directly injecting the Router.
tip: Router state becomes just another slice of your Store.
code:
StoreRouterConnectingModule.forRoot();

this.store.select(selectRouteParams);

question: What CRUD methods does the NgRx Entity Adapter provide?
answer: The Entity Adapter provides helper methods like addOne(), addMany(), setAll(), updateOne(), upsertOne(), removeOne(), and removeMany(). These methods perform immutable updates automatically.
tip: Using Entity Adapter removes the need to manually update arrays immutably.

question: What is the difference between withLatestFrom() and combineLatest() inside NgRx Effects?
answer: withLatestFrom() emits only when the source Observable emits, combining it with the latest values from other Observables. combineLatest() emits whenever any input Observable emits after all have emitted once. In Effects, withLatestFrom() is usually preferred to combine an Action with Store data.
tip: Effects usually start from an Action, so withLatestFrom() is the better choice.

question: What is dispatch: false in NgRx Effects and when should you use it?
answer: By default, every Effect dispatches a new Action. Setting dispatch: false tells NgRx that the Effect performs only a side effect (such as navigation, logging, or showing a toast) and should not dispatch another Action.
tip: Use dispatch: false only when you don't need to update the Store.
